key_pathways: C. difficile toxin-mediated glucosylation of GTP-binding proteins (Rho, Rac, Cdc42) leading to actin condensation, cell rounding, cytokine secretion, and cell death
Gut barrier integrity pathway (tight junction regulation, sCD14 secretion)
Short-chain fatty acid metabolism (butyrate production and its role in gut barrier strengthening and anti-inflammation)
Bile acid metabolism (secondary bile acids inhibiting CDI)

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Probiotic cocktail (Pro11) inhibits C. difficile growth and biofilm formation in vitro through metabolites such as organic acids and bacteriocins.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Co-culture experiments showed9 of11 strains significantly inhibited C. difficile growth; cell-free supernatants from all11 strains inhibited C. difficile biofilm formation (Figure1B,1C).

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Probiotic cocktail reduces C. difficile burden and toxin production in vivo, leading to lower clinical scores and higher survival.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Fecal C. difficile burden was33.7% of control and toxin concentration was30.9% of control in Pro11-fed mice (Figure2C,2D); clinical scores were significantly lower and survival rate was80% vs40% (Figure2A,2B).

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Probiotic cocktail strengthens gut barrier integrity and reduces gut permeability and inflammation during CDI.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Histological analysis showed intact mucus layer and villi in Pro11-fed mice vs disrupted villi and neutrophil infiltration in control; serum sCD14 was significantly lower in Pro11 group (Figure3B); IL-1β expression was significantly decreased in colon tissue (Figure3C).

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Probiotic cocktail increases gut microbiome diversity and enriches beneficial bacteria (Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium, Bacillus) while reducing Enterococcus, which may promote C. difficile fitness.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Alpha diversity was higher in Pro11-fed mice; LEfSe analysis showed enrichment of Bifidobacteriaceae, Lactobacillaceae, Bacillaceae; genus-level analysis showed increased Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium, Bacillus and decreased Enterococcus (Figure4, Supplementary Figure S2).

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Probiotic cocktail modulates gut metabolome, increasing butyrate and other beneficial metabolites that inhibit C. difficile and support gut health.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: PCA showed distinct metabolite clusters; significantly increased metabolites included butyrate,2-hydroxyisobutyrate, glutamate, leucine, valine; formate and acetate were decreased (Figure5). Butyrate is known to inhibit C. difficile and strengthen gut barrier.

key_findings: A human-origin probiotic cocktail (Pro11) containing11 strains (Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium, Streptococcus) was developed and shown to protect mice against CDI.
Pro11 reduced clinical scores, increased survival, and decreased C. difficile burden and toxin levels in feces.
Pro11 strengthened gut barrier integrity, reduced gut permeability (lower sCD14), and decreased colonic inflammation (lower IL-1β).
Pro11 increased gut microbiome diversity and beneficial bacteria (Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium, Bacillus) while reducing Enterococcus.
Pro11 increased gut metabolites including butyrate,2-hydroxyisobutyrate, glutamate, leucine, and valine, while decreasing formate and acetate.
The study highlights probiotics as a promising non-invasive therapy for CDI via gut microbiome-gut metabolome modulation.
relevance_to_research_topic: This paper provides a detailed framework for studying how gut microbiome and metabolome modulation affects CDI susceptibility and severity, using a probiotic intervention model. Although the research topic focuses on high-iron diet, this paper offers relevant background on CDI pathogenesis, key metabolites (e.g., butyrate) that influence C. difficile fitness, and methodologies for assessing gut barrier function, inflammation, microbial diversity, and metabolomics. The concepts of 'diet-microbiome-host' interaction are applicable, though iron is not addressed. The findings can serve as a comparative reference for understanding how dietary iron might similarly or differentially impact CDI through microbiota and metabolite changes.
